messages.content: I don't want to return values in the parameter, I want to separate different functions if needed but return the values by function instead
OK I added the text size but I want to implement TTF_MeasureUTF8 too, to Calculate how much of a UTF-8 string will fit in a given width., but I don't know how to name it

Yes it does exist, is a new function, here is the documentation:
TTF_MeasureUTF8
Calculate how much of a UTF-8 string will fit in a given width.

Syntax
int TTF_MeasureUTF8(TTF_Font *font, const char *text, int measure_width, int *extent, int *count);
Function Parameters
font	the font to query.
text	text to calculate, in UTF-8 encoding.
measure_width	maximum width, in pixels, available for the string.
count	on return, filled with number of characters that can be rendered.
extent	on return, filled with latest calculated width.
Return Value
Returns 0 if successful, -1 on error.
Separate this method into two, and don't use pair but return an int in each newly functions

OK that one is done, now I wanna add the Direction, Style and Hinting as structs, since I wanna wrap: TTF_SetFontStyle, TTF_SetFontDirection,. Can these structs be inside the class as they are closely related to the Font class?

Since I stored a font with all it's sizes in a map, and I treat every size as a different font object of the same font, should I implement TTF_SetFontSize to dynamically change the size? I don't know if this should be recommended by the way I did it, for example if there is only one size, then resizing it means I will have to change the Map to take the size out and add the new size, but also to check if there is another size with the same size, dunno if this is really correct

It is supported in the latest version:

TTF_SetFontSize
Set a font's size dynamically.

Syntax
int TTF_SetFontSize(TTF_Font *font, int ptsize);
Function Parameters
font	the font to resize.
ptsize	the new point size.
Return Value
Returns 0 if successful, -1 on error

Remarks
This clears already-generated glyphs, if any, from the cache.

Version
This function is available since SDL_ttf 2.0.18.
What if we make a single method that takes a boolean flag like anti-aliasing or quality, etc. and then use them to call the appropiate method instead?
Can it be done with simple bool variables instead of an enum class?
Should the flag be named blended or should we change the name to antiAlising? Which one is more intuitive?

The choice between `blended` and `antiAliasing` for the flag name depends on the level of detail you want to convey about what the rendering operation is doing, and also on how familiar your users are with the specifics of SDL_ttf and text rendering techniques.

In SDL_ttf, `TTF_RenderText_Blended` produces text with anti-aliasing and uses alpha blending to smoothly blend the edges of the text with the background. Meanwhile, `TTF_RenderText_Solid` renders the text without anti-aliasing, resulting in jagged edges but with faster performance.

Choosing the Flag Name:
- **blended**: This term is specific to the SDL_ttf naming convention. It indicates that the text will be rendered with anti-aliasing and alpha blending for a smooth appearance. It directly corresponds to the `TTF_RenderText_Blended` method provided by SDL_ttf.
- **antiAliasing**: This is a more general term for the technique used to smooth out edges in digital images and is not specific to SDL_ttf. It indicates that the text will be rendered with smoothed edges. It may be more intuitive for those who are not familiar with SDL_ttf specifics but still have some understanding of graphics rendering.

Here's a brief analysis:

- If your target audience is likely to be familiar with SDL_ttf and the specifics of text rendering techniques, using `blended` might be clearer since it directly corresponds to the method name in SDL_ttf.
- If you expect your users not to be intimately familiar with SDL_ttf or if you prioritize general graphics terms over library-specific ones, `antiAliasing` might be the more intuitive choice as it succinctly describes the visual effect rather than the specific rendering method.

In the interest of making your API intuitive and self-documenting, if you believe your users will better understand the term `antiAliasing`, then that would be the more user-friendly choice. On the other hand, if your users are expected to have SDL-specific knowledge, then `blended` could work well.

As a final note, it's important to document whatever term you choose clearly explaining the effect it has on the text rendering so that users can easily understand what to expect from each option.

Ok I will name it antiAliasing as I don't expect my users to be familiar with SDL. Now I was reading the names and found out there are UTF8 variants, and my fonts are all UTF8 by default, should I change the method to use UTF8 instead?
I plan on adding internalization to my project, like using different alphabets, etc. But what if someone uses ASCII only, would that be a problem with UTF8 methods

Using UTF-8 methods for text rendering when your text is ASCII-only will not be a problem. UTF-8 is backward compatible with ASCII, meaning that any valid ASCII text is also valid UTF-8 text. The first 128 characters of UTF-8 correspond directly one-to-one with ASCII characters.

UTF-8 was explicitly designed this way to ensure that systems could handle both ASCII and more extensive character sets without the need for different encoding schemes. This means you can render ASCII with UTF-8 rendering methods without any issues. 

In other words, if you have text that consists solely of ASCII characters and you use UTF-8 rendering functions in SDL_ttf (like `TTF_RenderUTF8_Solid`, `TTF_RenderUTF8_Shaded`, or `TTF_RenderUTF8_Blended`), these functions will process the text just as they would with any UTF-8 encoded string, and the end result will be the same as if you had used the non-UTF-8 variants.

This makes using the UTF-8 functions a safe and future-proof choice for your engine, as it supports both simple ASCII text and other languages and scripts that might be used in localization and internationalization efforts. It ensures that your game engine is ready for globalization without the need for conditional logic based on whether the text is ASCII or not.

Therefore, by standardizing on UTF-8 methods for rendering, you simplify your codebase and eliminate the need to manage different encoding schemes, streamlining your internationalization process.
